topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, the subject line a stark, unwelcome formality: "Regarding your recent piece, 'The Urban Echoes.'" My fingers hovered over the screen, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes. The article, my meticulously crafted analysis of city development, was undeniably mine, yet the byline beneath the headline read, in bold, arrogant script, "By Julian Vance." A slow, creeping heat began to rise in my chest, spreading outward until my palms felt clammy. I reread the text, each sentence a deliberate stab of disbelief. I stood up abruptly, pacing the cramped confines of my office, the cheap carpet muffling my frantic steps. When I finally sat back down, my jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscle twitching beneath my skin. I stared at the screen, the realization settling in like a physical weight, heavy and suffocating.

[story 2]

I found out when I received the notification from the publisher—a terse, almost apologetic message that managed to sound utterly dismissive. My breath hitched. I stared at the screen, the carefully constructed facade of professional composure crumbling around the edges. It was the article, the one I poured weeks into, now branded with another person's name. A sudden, sharp wave of nausea washed over me, making the room spin slightly. I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to physically block out the stark reality. When I finally lowered my hands, my voice, when I called my editor, came out thin and reedy, stripped bare of its usual confidence. "Is... is this correct?" The silence on the other end was thick, heavy with unspoken acknowledgment of the error, and it felt like a spotlight had suddenly been switched on, exposing every flawed detail of my recent work.

[story 3]

The morning started with a strange, hollow feeling in my core. I glanced at my laptop, expecting the usual flow of updates, but instead, a single, glaring notification dominated the display. My blood ran cold as I read the attribution. My name was absent; Julian Vance’s name was front and center, claiming ownership of my intellectual property. A strange, shaky laugh escaped my lips, quickly dissolving into a tight, painful grimace. I walked over to the window, the city outside seeming too bright, too indifferent to the internal turmoil churning within me. Every muscle in my neck felt tight, coiled and ready to spring. I walked back to my desk, my movements stiff, and slowly, deliberately, I deleted the file, the action feeling less like a choice and more like a desperate attempt to erase a very public mistake.
